title: Mums’ desperate calls highlight housing crisis
full_text:
Phone calls from Far North mothers desperate for a place to stay — so they don’t have to shelter with their children under bridges overnight — aren’t uncommon, a local charity says.
The insight from Rongopai House Community Trust executive officer Dino Houtas comes amid new research showing Northland children are among those most affected by a growing homelessness crisis.
The latest research by the Coalition to End Women’s Homelessness estimates more than 33,000 children are experiencing severe housing deprivation, including overcrowding, couch surfing, temporary accommodation and unsafe or uninhabitable homes.
Drawing on Integrated Data Infrastructure from the 2023 Census, the report by analytics firm Taylor Fry provides insight into the experiences of 33,192 children and young people.
Of those, 1716 children and young people are from Northland.
The findings show children and young people experiencing homelessness face higher rates of harm across many areas of their lives. They are nearly three times more likely to have experienced abuse, to disengage from education, have poor health outcomes, and interact with the justice system than other children.
The report also found children under 5 experiencing homelessness are less likely to be connected to foundational services such as primary health organisations and early childhood education, and are more likely to experience preventable hospitalisations, including respiratory illness and preventable disease.
Rongopai House Community Trust executive officer Dino Houtas said resources in the Far North are scarce and mothers are struggling to house their children.
“There just isn’t housing. We’ve had, unfortunately, on many instances, emergency phone calls, mums coming up and saying, ‘if I don’t get somewhere to live tonight, I’m going to have to go and sleep under the bridge with my children’.
“That sort of thing, unfortunately, is quite common,” he said.
Houtas said local agencies do their best to assist with emergency housing, but it is not easy and pressures continue to mount.
“If people are engaged with us, we’re happy to support them in getting accommodation and work on their tenancy agreements and maybe be co-signatories and that sort of thing. But housing is a massive shortage.”
Whare Āwhina chief executive Liz Cassidy-Canning said high transience rates at Northland schools show the impact of child homelessness.
She said unaffordable or unstable housing has contributed to homelessness. The Whangārei-based kaupapa Māori support provider speaks to struggling families living in overcrowded conditions because they were unable to secure a tenancy.
“Many are living in garages and cold, damp spaces while searching for long-term options.
“We also see young people every day couch surfing or living rough due to relationship breakdowns and unsafe conditions.”
Cassidy-Canning said whānau do their best with their limited income and housing options but, sadly, children are living in substandard housing.
Dire housing instability for children, coupled with food insecurity, affected learning, behaviour, and long-term health and wellbeing, she said.
The Ministry of Social Development’s social housing register showed 1020 Northland families with an A or B housing priority — a serious housing need — as of March 2026.
“Children should not have to experience this stress at such a young age.
“Our investment as a country needs to prioritise the needs of our children so they can thrive; a warm, dry, safe home will make all the difference,” she said.
Cassidy-Canning believed Te Tai Tokerau was an “afterthought”, with limited investment in housing and infrastructure.
Dr Jo Cribb, chair of the Wellington Homeless Women’s Trust Te Whare Nukunoa and member of the Coalition to End Women’s Homelessness, said growing up without the safety, stability and sense of home every child has a right to was a “horrible” reality for many.
“We’re talking babies, toddlers without shelters, babies in temporary accommodation, teenagers packed into bedrooms. The cost on children is their childhood.”
In Northland she said homelessness has been compounded by climate-related disasters.
“After floods, heavy rains, there isn’t a whole lot of housing stock waiting for people to move into.”
Cribb believed there was a lack of “good” government support and resourcing that helped whānau recover from natural disasters.
Housing Minister Chris Bishop has previously said official reporting shows homelessness is increasing and “it’s clear we have a real problem”.
The Government says it is responding through a multi-year reform of the social housing system announced in Budget 2026, aimed at better targeting people with the most severe housing need and increasing access to social housing.
NZME Publishing Ltd.
